scenarios.task: We have $150K approved for this quarter's campaign. My plan is to figure out how to spend the full $150K effectively across the channels we usually use, then set our reach and frequency expectations based on what that budget buys.
criteria: Flags starting from the budget rather than the objective as the budget-first-planning failure mode
Recommends starting from the specific campaign objective and target audience, determining the reach/frequency/channel mix that objective requires, then checking cost against budget — not the reverse
States that starting from 'how do we spend this' tends to produce a plan optimized for spend rather than outcome
Asks what the specific objective and audience are before accepting the budget-driven planning approach

scenarios.task: The creative brief I'm sending the agency says: 'Promote our brand broadly to raise awareness and appeal to as many potential customers as possible, showcasing our full range of products.' I think giving them a wide mandate like this gives the creative team maximum room to be creative.
criteria: Flags this as a vague creative brief, naming under-specification of audience and message as the failure mode, not creative freedom
States that a broad, unfocused brief produces work that tries to appeal to everyone and lands with no one specifically
Recommends tightening the brief to one specific audience, one core message, and one desired action
Distinguishes over-specifying execution (bad) from under-specifying the who/what/why (also bad) — the fix is specificity on audience/message, not dictating visuals

scenarios.task: Our ad platform's dashboard shows this channel drove 2,400 conversions last month with a strong last-click attribution model, so I'm planning to shift more budget into it since it's clearly our best-performing channel by a wide margin.
criteria: Flags trusting the platform's self-reported last-click attribution at face value as a failure mode
States that last-click and platform-reported attribution systematically over-credit the channel closest to conversion
Recommends validating with a holdout test or incrementality check before reallocating significant budget based on this number alone
Notes the specific conflict of interest — the platform reporting the number benefits from the budget shift its own numbers justify

scenarios.task: One channel has been performing really well for us for over a year, so we've concentrated almost all of our paid budget there since it's clearly the highest-return option and there's no reason to spread spend into weaker-performing channels.
criteria: Flags heavy concentration in a single channel as the single-channel-overreliance failure mode, even given strong current performance
States the specific risks this creates — cost inflation, algorithm changes, or audience fatigue in that one channel — that a diversified plan would avoid
Recommends maintaining some channel diversity as risk management, not purely as a hedge against underperformance
Doesn't argue against the channel's current strong performance, only against concentrating essentially all spend there
